findings.description: Session tokens are generated with random.choice() from the non-cryptographic Mersenne Twister PRNG (CWE-330/CWE-338); ~624 observed tokens let an attacker reconstruct PRNG state and predict all future tokens, enabling session hijacking — must use the secrets module.

touch() slides expiry forward without checking expires_at, so a logically-expired session can be resurrected/kept alive indefinitely whenever touch is called before a get()/active_count() purge runs (CWE-613 Insufficient Session Expiration).

No absolute session-lifetime cap combined with sliding renewal in touch() means a token can be renewed forever, and create() has no limit on session count so repeated calls grow _sessions unboundedly (expired entries reaped only lazily) — memory-exhaustion DoS (CWE-770) and indefinite session lifetime (CWE-613).

The store is not thread-safe: _purge_expired() iterates/mutates self._sessions while create()/destroy() can mutate it concurrently, risking 'dictionary changed size during iteration' RuntimeError on the auth path (availability/DoS) and inconsistent expiry state (CWE-362).

user_sessions() skips _purge_expired() so it can return already-expired Session objects (stale-credential read), and it returns raw token values directly, risking credential exposure if the result is logged or serialized (CWE-200/CWE-613).
